cards.text: The {{c1::protected}} access specifier allows the class the member belongs to, {{c2::friends}}, and {{c3::derived classes}} to access the member.

cards.front: What are the three types of inheritance in C++?

cards.text:
To inherit publicly: {{c1::class Derived: public Base}}
To inherit privately: {{c2::class Derived: private Base}}
To inherit protectedly: {{c3::class Derived: protected Base}}

cards.front: What is the default inheritance type in C++ if you don't specify public, protected, or private?
cards.back: Private inheritance (just like members default to private access if you don't specify otherwise).

cards.question: With public inheritance, what access level do inherited public members have in the derived class?
cards.explanation: With public inheritance, inherited public members stay public, and inherited protected members stay protected.

With public inheritance:
  • Public members → stay public
  • Protected members → stay protected
  • Private members → inaccessible
examples: class Derived: public Base { }; // Most common
Derived d; d.m_public = 1; // OK if m_public was public in Base

cards.text: You should use {{c1::public}} inheritance unless you have a specific reason to do otherwise. It is by far the {{c2::most commonly}} used type of inheritance.

cards.front: With private inheritance, what access level do inherited public and protected members have in the derived class?
cards.back: They both become private in the derived class.

cards.front: With protected inheritance, what access level do inherited public members have in the derived class?
cards.back: They become protected in the derived class.

cards.front: When should you use the protected access specifier?
cards.back: When you're deriving from your own classes, the number of derived classes is reasonable, and you want derived classes to have direct access to base members without a large interface overhead.

cards.front: What is the main disadvantage of using protected members in a base class?
cards.back: If you change the protected member (type, meaning, etc.), you'll probably need to change both the base class AND all derived classes, increasing maintenance burden.

cards.question: Can the public access m_public through a Derived object with private inheritance?
cards.explanation: With private inheritance, m_public becomes private in Derived, so the public cannot access it through a Derived object.

cards.front: Do the access rules for inherited members apply to member functions as well as member variables?
cards.back: Yes, these access rules hold true for all members, including member functions and types declared inside the class.
